mcqs.question: What is the primary purpose of a cache in a computer system?

options.text: To reduce the latency of memory access by storing frequently used data closer to the CPU.
To permanently store the operating system and user files.
To provide a large, slow storage area for archival purposes.
To manage the power consumption of the main processor.

mcqs.explanation: A cache is a small, fast memory unit located close to the CPU. Its main goal is to hold copies of frequently accessed data from main memory (RAM) to avoid the slower process of fetching it from RAM on every request, thereby reducing effective access time.

mcqs.question: In a multi-core processor with a shared L3 cache and private L1/L2 caches, which cache coherence protocol state indicates that a cache line is clean and may be shared by multiple caches?

options.text: Shared
Exclusive
Modified
Invalid

mcqs.explanation: In the MESI protocol, the 'Shared' state means the cache line is clean (identical to main memory) and other processors may also have a copy of it. This is a common state in read-only or read-frequently data scenarios.

mcqs.question: What is the term for the situation where multiple processors repeatedly invalidate and update a shared cache line, causing it to 'ping-pong' between caches and severely degrading performance?

options.text: False Sharing
Cache Thrashing
Cache Coherence
Memory Stalling

mcqs.explanation: Cache thrashing occurs when multiple cores need frequent, exclusive access to the same memory location (or same cache line). This causes a storm of coherence messages (invalidations and updates) as the line is constantly moved between cores, wasting bandwidth and slowing down execution.

mcqs.question: A program exhibits poor performance on a multi-core system due to false sharing. Which of the following strategies is most effective at mitigating this issue?

options.text: Increasing the overall size of the L1 cache.
Aligning data and padding memory elements so that variables used by different threads reside on separate cache lines.
Converting all shared variables to be declared with the 'volatile' keyword.
Increasing the clock speed of the CPU to process coherence messages faster.

mcqs.explanation: False sharing occurs when unrelated variables used by different threads accidentally reside on the same cache line. When one thread writes to its variable, it invalidates the entire cache line for all cores. Padding and alignment ensure each thread's frequently accessed data is on its own cache line, preventing unnecessary invalidations.

mcqs.question: Considering a directory-based cache coherence protocol, what is the fundamental advantage it has over a snooping-based protocol when scaling to a large number of processor cores?

options.text: It uses a centralized directory to track the state of cache lines, eliminating the broadcast traffic that becomes a bottleneck in large snooping systems.
It allows for higher processor clock speeds due to simpler coherence logic.
It requires no additional hardware beyond the standard cache controllers present in each core.
It guarantees lower latency for all memory operations compared to any snooping protocol.

mcqs.explanation: Snooping protocols rely on broadcasting coherence requests to all cores, which creates immense bus or interconnect traffic as the system scales up. A directory-based protocol maintains a central directory that knows which cores have a copy of a cache line. It can send point-to-point messages only to those specific sharers, drastically reducing bandwidth consumption and enabling scalability to many cores.
